knowledge.what_it_is: Data engineering is the process of designing, building, and maintaining the infrastructure that enables organizations to collect, store, transform, and analyze large datasets. It involves creating data pipelines, architectures, and systems that can handle high-volume, high-velocity, and high-variety data. Data engineers work closely with data scientists, analysts, and other stakeholders to ensure that data is accurate, reliable, and easily accessible for business insights.
what_you_will_learn: Data warehousing — designing and implementing a centralized repository for storing and managing large datasets
ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) pipelines — creating automated processes for extracting data from various sources, transforming it into a standardized format, and loading it into a target system
Data ingestion — designing and implementing systems for collecting and processing high-volume, high-velocity data from various sources such as IoT devices, social media, or logs
Data governance — establishing policies, procedures, and standards for managing data quality, security, and compliance across the organization
Cloud-native architectures — designing and building scalable, secure, and highly available systems using cloud-based services such as AWS Lambda, Google Cloud Functions, or Azure Functions
Apache Beam — using a unified programming model for processing large datasets in batch or streaming mode on various execution engines such as Apache Flink, Apache Spark, or Google Cloud Dataflow
Data quality and validation — implementing techniques for ensuring data accuracy, completeness, and consistency across the organization

knowledge.what_it_is: Data engineering and data science are two distinct disciplines within the field of data, with different goals, methodologies, and tools. Data engineers design, build, and maintain large-scale data systems, while data scientists focus on extracting insights from data using machine learning algorithms.
what_you_will_learn: Data Engineering vs Data Science — understanding the key differences in roles, responsibilities, and deliverables
Data Ingestion Pipelines — designing scalable pipelines for ingesting data from various sources (e.g., Apache Beam, AWS Kinesis)
Data Warehousing Architectures — choosing between star schema, snowflake schema, and denormalized schema designs
ETL vs ELT — understanding the trade-offs between extract-transform-load and extract-load-transform approaches
Data Quality Metrics — designing and implementing metrics for data quality, such as data completeness, accuracy, and consistency
Data Governance Frameworks — establishing frameworks for data governance, including data lineage, data provenance, and data access control
Cloud-Native Data Engineering — designing data systems for cloud-native environments (e.g., AWS Lambda, Google Cloud Functions)
Real-Time Data Processing — implementing real-time data processing using tools like Apache Flink, Apache Storm, or Amazon Kinesis

knowledge.what_it_is: A Data Engineer designs, builds, and maintains large-scale data processing systems to extract insights from complex datasets. They ensure data quality, integrity, and security while optimizing system performance.
what_you_will_learn: Data modeling — creating conceptual and logical models of data structures to define relationships between entities
ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) pipelines — designing and implementing processes for extracting data from sources, transforming it into a usable format, and loading it into target systems
Data warehousing — architecting and implementing data warehouses using tools like Amazon Redshift, Google BigQuery, or Snowflake
Big Data processing frameworks — working with Hadoop, Spark, Flink, or other distributed computing frameworks to process large datasets
Cloud-based data platforms — designing and deploying cloud-native data platforms on AWS, GCP, Azure, or other cloud providers
Data governance — implementing policies and procedures for data security, access control, and compliance with regulations like GDPR, HIPAA, or CCPA
Data quality assurance — developing and executing strategies to ensure data accuracy, completeness, and consistency across systems
Monitoring and logging — setting up monitoring tools like Prometheus, Grafana, or New Relic to track system performance and log data

knowledge.what_it_is: Choosing the right technologies as a Data Engineer involves selecting tools and frameworks that align with project requirements, scalability needs, and team expertise. This process ensures efficient data processing, storage, and analysis while minimizing costs and complexity.
what_you_will_learn: Evaluating NoSQL databases — MongoDB, Cassandra, Couchbase, RavenDB: trade-offs between ACID compliance, scalability, and query flexibility
Choosing a cloud provider — AWS, GCP, Azure, Alibaba Cloud: considering pricing models, data transfer costs, and region availability
Selecting a data warehousing solution — Amazon Redshift, Google BigQuery, Snowflake: balancing performance, cost, and ease of use
Assessing big data processing frameworks — Apache Spark, Hadoop, Flink: evaluating scalability, fault tolerance, and integration with existing tools
Determining the best ETL tool for data integration — Talend, Informatica, AWS Glue: considering data quality, transformation complexity, and performance requirements
Understanding containerization for data workloads — Docker, Kubernetes: ensuring efficient resource utilization, scalability, and high availability

knowledge.what_it_is: Programming skills are essential for Data Engineers to design, develop, and maintain large-scale data systems. It involves writing efficient, scalable, and reliable code using various programming languages.
what_you_will_learn: Variables and data types — understanding the difference between primitive and reference types in programming languages
Control structures — loops (for, while, do-while), conditional statements (if-else, switch), and functions
Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) concepts — classes, objects, inheritance, polymorphism, encapsulation, and abstraction
Functions as first-class citizens — passing functions as arguments to other functions, returning functions from functions
Lambda functions and closures — concise function definitions and accessing outer scope variables
Error handling and debugging techniques — try-catch blocks, logging, and debugging tools (e.g., print statements, debuggers)
Memory management and garbage collection — understanding how programming languages manage memory and resources
Concurrency and parallelism — writing efficient concurrent code using threads, processes, or async/await

knowledge.what_it_is: Python is a high-level, interpreted programming language widely used in data engineering for tasks such as data processing, ETL (Extract, Transform, Load), and data science. It has extensive libraries like Pandas, NumPy, and scikit-learn that make it an ideal choice for data engineers.
what_you_will_learn: Python syntax basics — understanding indentation, variables, control structures, functions
Data types in Python — working with integers, floats, strings, lists, dictionaries, sets
Pandas library fundamentals — creating and manipulating DataFrames, Series, and Index objects
NumPy array operations — performing vectorized operations on arrays, broadcasting, indexing
Data cleaning and preprocessing techniques using Pandas and NumPy — handling missing values, data normalization, feature scaling
Working with CSV and JSON files in Python — reading, writing, and manipulating file formats
Regular expressions in Python — pattern matching, searching, replacing text
Python decorators for code reuse — implementing caching, logging, authentication mechanisms
Asyncio library basics — working with coroutines, asynchronous functions, event loops
AIOHTTP library fundamentals — making HTTP requests asynchronously using async/await syntax
Using Python's built-in data structures — lists, dictionaries, sets for efficient data storage and retrieval
Data serialization and deserialization in Python — using Pickle, JSON, and MessagePack formats
Working with databases in Python — connecting to MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQLite using libraries like SQLAlchemy
Implementing ETL processes in Python — extracting data from sources, transforming it into a usable format, loading it into a target system

knowledge.what_it_is: Java is an object-oriented, platform-independent programming language used extensively in enterprise software development and data engineering applications. It's designed to be compiled to bytecode that can run on any Java Virtual Machine (JVM), making it a popular choice for building scalable and maintainable systems.
what_you_will_learn: Java fundamentals — understanding classes, objects, inheritance, polymorphism, encapsulation, and abstraction
Java collections framework — working with ArrayList, LinkedList, HashMap, HashSet, TreeSet, and other data structures
Java concurrency — using threads, locks, semaphores, and concurrent collections for parallelism and synchronization
Java streams API — processing data in a declarative way using lambda expressions and method references
Java persistence API (JPA) — mapping Java objects to relational databases using Hibernate or EclipseLink
Java Data Objects (JDO) — defining and persisting Java classes as database tables
Apache Commons Lang, BeanUtils, and Validator — leveraging utility libraries for string manipulation, bean property management, and validation
Java 8 features — understanding lambda expressions, method references, functional interfaces, and default methods
Java 11+ features — taking advantage of var keyword, switch expression, and other modern language enhancements

knowledge.what_it_is: Scala is a multi-paradigm programming language designed to be concise and elegant, with strong support for functional programming and object-oriented programming. It runs on the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) and is widely used in data engineering for its high-level abstractions and concise syntax.
what_you_will_learn: Functional Programming — Scala's core concept, using immutable values, higher-order functions, and closures
Object-Oriented Programming — Scala's support for classes, objects, inheritance, and polymorphism
Pattern Matching — a powerful feature for conditional expressions and type-safe dispatching
Type Systems — Scala's advanced type inference and type parameters for generic programming
Closures and Higher-Order Functions — using functions as first-class citizens and passing them as arguments
Immutable Data Structures — using immutable collections, such as List and Map, to ensure thread-safety
Actor Model — a concurrency model for building scalable and fault-tolerant systems
Akka Framework — a popular toolkit for building concurrent and distributed systems in Scala
Scala Collections API — working with immutable and mutable collections, including lists, sets, and maps
Type Classes and Traits — using type classes to define reusable behavior and traits to mix-in functionality

knowledge.what_it_is: Go, also known as Golang, is a statically typed, compiled language developed by Google. It's designed to be simple and efficient, with a focus on concurrency and parallelism. Go is widely used for building scalable, distributed systems, network servers, and cloud infrastructure.
what_you_will_learn: Go Modules — managing dependencies using the go.mod file
goroutine — lightweight threads for concurrent execution of tasks
channel — communication mechanism between goroutines using buffered or unbuffered channels
select statement — non-blocking channel operations and timeouts
interface — defining methods without implementing them, used for abstraction and polymorphism
context package — managing request-scoped values and cancellation signals
net/http package — building HTTP servers and clients with Go's standard library
database/sql package — interacting with relational databases using the SQL driver interface
subtopics: go vet and go fmt tools

knowledge.what_it_is: Data structures and algorithms are fundamental concepts in computer science that enable efficient data processing, storage, and retrieval. As a data engineer, you will work with large datasets and develop scalable systems, making it essential to understand how to design and optimize data structures and algorithms for performance.
what_you_will_learn: Arrays — dynamic collections of elements that can be accessed using indices, commonly used in data processing pipelines
Linked lists — sequential data structures where each element points to the next one, useful for implementing stacks and queues
Stacks — last-in-first-out (LIFO) data structures that use arrays or linked lists to store and retrieve elements
Queues — first-in-first-out (FIFO) data structures that use arrays or linked lists to store and retrieve elements
Trees — hierarchical data structures composed of nodes, used for efficient storage and retrieval of large datasets
Heaps — specialized trees that satisfy the heap property, commonly used in priority queue implementations
Hash tables — data structures that map keys to values using hash functions, providing fast lookup and insertion times
Graphs — non-linear data structures composed of nodes and edges, used for modeling complex relationships between data entities
Big O notation — a measure of algorithmic complexity, used to analyze the performance of algorithms in terms of time and space
Time complexity analysis — techniques for analyzing the running time of algorithms, including worst-case, average-case, and best-case scenarios
Space complexity analysis — techniques for analyzing the memory usage of algorithms, including worst-case, average-case, and best-case scenarios
Dynamic programming — a method for solving complex problems by breaking them down into smaller sub-problems and storing their solutions
Greedy algorithms — a class of algorithms that make locally optimal choices to solve a problem, often used for optimization problems
Divide and conquer — a strategy for solving complex problems by dividing them into smaller sub-problems and solving each one recursively
Sorting algorithms — techniques for arranging data in a specific order, including bubble sort, selection sort, merge sort, and quicksort
Searching algorithms — techniques for finding specific elements within a dataset, including linear search, binary search, and hash table lookup

knowledge.what_it_is: Git is a free and open-source version control system, widely used by software developers to track changes made to codebases. GitHub is a web-based platform that provides a central location for hosting and sharing Git repositories. As a Data Engineer, you will use Git and GitHub to collaborate with team members, manage code changes, and ensure data integrity.
what_you_will_learn: Git basics — understanding the core concepts of Git, including commits, branches, merges, and push/pull operations
GitHub repositories — creating, managing, and collaborating on remote repositories using GitHub's web interface and API
Branching strategies — implementing feature branching, release branching, and hotfix branching to manage code changes and deployments
Git flow — understanding the Git flow workflow, including features, releases, and hotfixes, to manage complex software development projects
GitHub Actions — automating build, test, and deployment processes using GitHub's built-in CI/CD tool
Git hooks — customizing Git behavior with pre-commit, commit-msg, and post-commit hooks to enforce coding standards and automate tasks
GitHub Pages — hosting static websites directly from GitHub repositories using GitHub Pages
Git submodules — managing dependencies between projects by including other Git repositories as submodules

knowledge.what_it_is: The Data Engineering Lifecycle encompasses the end-to-end process of designing, building, deploying, and maintaining data pipelines, systems, and architectures that support business intelligence, analytics, and decision-making. It involves a range of activities from data ingestion to data serving, with a focus on scalability, reliability, and performance.
what_you_will_learn: Data Ingestion — designing and implementing data pipelines for collecting, processing, and loading data into target systems (e.g., Apache Beam, Spark Streaming)

Data Processing — transforming, aggregating, and enriching data using various techniques such as batch processing (Hadoop MapReduce), stream processing (Apache Flink), or real-time processing (Apache Storm)
Data Storage — selecting and implementing suitable storage solutions for raw and processed data (e.g., relational databases, NoSQL databases, data warehouses, data lakes)
Data Serving — designing and deploying APIs, interfaces, and services to provide access to data for various stakeholders (e.g., Tableau, Power BI, custom dashboards)
Data Quality — implementing data validation, cleansing, and normalization techniques to ensure data accuracy and consistency
Data Governance — establishing policies, procedures, and standards for data management, security, and compliance

knowledge.what_it_is: Data generation refers to the process of creating synthetic data that mimics real-world data distributions, used in various applications such as testing, training machine learning models, and data augmentation. As a Data Engineer, you will be responsible for designing and implementing data generation pipelines.
what_you_will_learn: Synthetic data generation — using techniques like generative adversarial networks (GANs), variational autoencoders (VAEs), and probabilistic programming to create realistic data
Data augmentation — applying transformations such as rotation, scaling, and flipping to existing data to increase its diversity and size
Data sampling — selecting a subset of data from a larger dataset to represent the population, using techniques like stratified sampling and random sampling
Data anonymization — removing personally identifiable information (PII) from real-world data to make it usable for training machine learning models or testing applications
Data masking — replacing sensitive information with fictional data while preserving the original distribution of values
Open-source data generation tools — using libraries like Faker, Datagen, and Dask to generate synthetic data in various formats such as CSV, JSON, and Parquet
Custom data generation pipelines — designing and implementing end-to-end data generation workflows using technologies like Apache Beam, Apache Spark, and AWS Glue

knowledge.what_it_is: Data ingestion refers to the process of collecting, processing, and loading data from various sources into a centralized system or database for analysis, reporting, and decision-making. As a Data Engineer, you will be responsible for designing, implementing, and maintaining efficient data ingestion pipelines that meet business requirements.
what_you_will_learn: Apache NiFi — a popular open-source tool for building data ingestion pipelines with real-time processing capabilities
Apache Beam — a unified programming model for both batch and streaming data processing, supporting multiple execution engines like Flink, Spark, and MapReduce
Kafka Connect — a tool for integrating Apache Kafka with external systems, enabling efficient data ingestion from various sources
AWS Kinesis Data Firehose — a fully managed service for loading data into Amazon S3, Redshift, or Elasticsearch, providing real-time processing capabilities
Apache Flume — a distributed, reliable, and available system for efficiently collecting, aggregating, and moving large amounts of log data from various sources to HDFS or HBase
Data Lake architecture — designing a scalable and secure data lake using cloud storage services like AWS S3, Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2, or Google Cloud Storage
Change Data Capture (CDC) — implementing CDC mechanisms to capture changes in source databases and apply them to target systems for real-time data integration
Streaming ETL — processing streaming data in real-time using tools like Apache Flink, Apache Spark Streaming, or AWS Kinesis Analytics

knowledge.what_it_is: Data storage refers to the process of storing and managing data in a way that makes it easily accessible, scalable, and secure. As a Data Engineer, you will design, implement, and maintain data storage systems to support business intelligence, analytics, and machine learning workloads.
what_you_will_learn: Relational databases — designing and optimizing relational database schema for high-performance querying and data retrieval
NoSQL databases — choosing the right NoSQL database (e.g., MongoDB, Cassandra, Redis) for handling semi-structured or unstructured data
Data warehousing — designing and implementing data warehouses (e.g., Amazon Redshift, Google BigQuery) for business intelligence and analytics workloads
Column-store databases — understanding the benefits of column-store databases (e.g., Apache Cassandra, Amazon Aurora) for analytical queries
Graph databases — using graph databases (e.g., Neo4j, Amazon Neptune) to store and query complex relationships between data entities
Cloud-native storage solutions — designing and implementing cloud-native storage solutions (e.g., Amazon S3, Google Cloud Storage) for scalable and secure data storage
Data compression and encoding — optimizing data storage by using compression algorithms (e.g., gzip, Snappy) and encoding schemes (e.g., JSON, Avro)
Data partitioning and sharding — designing and implementing data partitioning and sharding strategies to improve query performance and scalability

knowledge.what_it_is: Data serving refers to the process of making data available to applications and users through various interfaces, such as APIs, web services, or file systems. As a Data Engineer, you will design and implement data serving architectures that meet the performance, scalability, and reliability requirements of your organization.
what_you_will_learn: API Gateway — designing and implementing API gateways to manage incoming requests, authenticate users, and route traffic to backend services
Service Mesh — building service meshes using tools like Istio or LinkerD to monitor, secure, and optimize microservices communication
Data Lake Storage — selecting and configuring data lake storage solutions like Amazon S3, Azure Data Lake Storage, or HDFS for efficient data serving
Caching Layers — designing caching layers using in-memory data grids like Hazelcast or Redis to reduce latency and improve performance
Content Delivery Networks (CDNs) — implementing CDNs to distribute static assets, reduce latency, and improve user experience
Serverless Computing — building serverless applications using AWS Lambda, Google Cloud Functions, or Azure Functions for scalable data serving
Data Virtualization — designing data virtualization layers using tools like Denodo or TIBCO to abstract complex data sources and provide a unified interface
Real-time Data Streaming — implementing real-time data streaming architectures using Apache Kafka, Amazon Kinesis, or Google Cloud Pub/Sub

knowledge.what_it_is: The CAP Theorem states that it's impossible to simultaneously achieve more than two out of three goals: Consistency, Availability, and Partition Tolerance. This theorem has significant implications for distributed systems design, particularly in data engineering.
what_you_will_learn: Consistency — the guarantee that all nodes in a distributed system will have the same view of the data
Availability — the ability to continue providing service even when some nodes fail or are partitioned from others
Partition Tolerance — the capacity for a distributed system to function correctly even when network partitions occur
CAP Theorem implications on distributed database design, including trade-offs between consistency and availability
Eventual Consistency — a weaker form of consistency where data may not be immediately consistent across all nodes
CA (Consistency-Availability) Trade-off — the decision to prioritize consistency or availability in a distributed system
PACELC Theorem — an extension of the CAP Theorem that considers the trade-offs between consistency, availability, and latency
Distributed database systems like Amazon Aurora, Google Cloud Spanner, and CockroachDB that implement various consistency models

knowledge.what_it_is: Horizontal and vertical scaling are two fundamental concepts in distributed systems, allowing data engineers to design scalable architectures that meet growing demands.
what_you_will_learn: Horizontal scaling — adding more nodes or servers to a cluster to increase capacity, load balancing using HAProxy, NGINX, or Amazon ELB
Vertical scaling — increasing the power of individual nodes by upgrading CPU, memory, or storage, using cloud providers like AWS EC2, Google Cloud Compute Engine, or Azure Virtual Machines
Scalability metrics — understanding CPU utilization, memory usage, I/O wait times, and network latency to determine when to scale up or out
Auto-scaling tools — configuring AWS Auto Scaling, Google Cloud Autoscaling, or Azure autoscale to automatically adjust resource allocation based on demand
Load balancing algorithms — implementing Round-Robin, Least Connection, IP Hash, or Session Persistence load balancing strategies using HAProxy, NGINX, or Amazon ELB
Distributed database design — applying horizontal partitioning (sharding), vertical partitioning (column-store), or denormalization techniques to optimize data distribution and retrieval

Cloud provider considerations — evaluating AWS RDS, Google Cloud SQL, or Azure Database Services for scalable relational databases, or using NoSQL solutions like Amazon DynamoDB, Google Cloud Bigtable, or Azure Cosmos DB

knowledge.what_it_is: A consensus protocol ensures that all nodes in a distributed system agree on the state of the system, even in the presence of network partitions or failures. This is crucial for data engineers to ensure data consistency and integrity across multiple nodes.
what_you_will_learn: Paxos algorithm — a widely used consensus protocol that guarantees agreement among nodes, even with faulty behavior
Raft consensus algorithm — a more modern and efficient alternative to Paxos, designed for high availability and scalability
Leader election — the process of selecting a leader node in a distributed system, responsible for making decisions and coordinating actions
Two-phase commit protocol — a technique used to ensure atomicity across multiple nodes, preventing partial commits or rollbacks
Distributed lock management — techniques for managing locks across multiple nodes, ensuring exclusive access to shared resources
Consensus protocols in NoSQL databases — how consensus protocols are implemented and optimized in popular NoSQL databases like Cassandra, Riak, and MongoDB
Fault tolerance and recovery mechanisms — strategies for handling node failures, network partitions, and other faults in distributed systems

knowledge.what_it_is: A database is a centralized repository of organized data, used by Data Engineers to store and manage structured or semi-structured data. It provides a way to efficiently retrieve and manipulate data using various querying languages.
what_you_will_learn: Database schema design — designing the logical structure of a database using entities, attributes, and relationships
Data modeling — creating a conceptual representation of the data using entity-relationship diagrams (ERDs) or data modeling tools like Lucidchart
Relational databases vs NoSQL databases — understanding the trade-offs between relational databases (e.g., MySQL, PostgreSQL) and NoSQL databases (e.g., MongoDB, Cassandra)
ACID properties — ensuring atomicity, consistency, isolation, and durability in database transactions using locking mechanisms or multi-version concurrency control
Database normalization — organizing data to minimize data redundancy and improve data integrity by applying normal forms (1NF, 2NF, 3NF)
Indexing strategies — optimizing query performance by creating indexes on columns used in WHERE, JOIN, and ORDER BY clauses
Data warehousing — designing a centralized repository for business intelligence and analytics using star or snowflake schema
Database replication — maintaining data consistency across multiple databases using techniques like master-slave replication or multi-master replication

knowledge.what_it_is: Logs are a critical component of data engineering, providing visibility into system performance, errors, and user interactions. They can be used to troubleshoot issues, monitor application health, and inform business decisions.
what_you_will_learn: Log levels — understanding the difference between debug, info, warning, error, and fatal log messages
Log formats — learning about common formats such as JSON, CSV, and Apache Log Format (ALF)
Log rotation and retention — implementing strategies for managing log file size and age
Centralized logging solutions — setting up tools like ELK Stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana), Splunk, or Sumo Logic
Log analysis and visualization — using libraries like Grafana, Promtail, or Fluentd to extract insights from logs
Security considerations for log data — understanding the importance of encryption, access control, and auditing
Log shipping and forwarding — implementing protocols like TCP, UDP, or syslog for sending logs between systems

knowledge.what_it_is: APIs (Application Programming Interfaces) are software intermediaries that enable different applications to communicate with each other, exchanging data and functionality. In a Data Engineering context, APIs play a crucial role in integrating various systems, services, and tools.
what_you_will_learn: RESTful API design — designing APIs using REST principles (resources, HTTP methods, URI structure) for scalable and maintainable integration
API Gateway patterns — implementing API gateways to manage traffic, security, and rate limiting for APIs
GraphQL vs REST — understanding the trade-offs between GraphQL and REST for data querying and manipulation
API Security best practices — securing APIs using OAuth, JWT, SSL/TLS, and input validation
API Documentation standards — documenting APIs using OpenAPI, Swagger, or API Blueprint for better understanding and reuse
API Versioning strategies — managing API versioning using URI, query parameter, or header-based approaches
API Caching mechanisms — implementing caching using Redis, Memcached, or in-memory caching to improve performance
API Rate Limiting techniques — limiting API requests using IP blocking, token bucket, or fixed window algorithms

knowledge.what_it_is: Mobile apps are software applications designed to run on mobile devices, such as smartphones and tablets. As a data engineer, you will work with mobile app data sources, including analytics, user behavior, and sensor data.
what_you_will_learn: Mobile app analytics frameworks — understanding Google Analytics for Firebase, Firebase Crashlytics, and other tools for tracking app performance and user engagement
Data ingestion from mobile apps — learning about APIs like Google Play Store's API, Apple App Store's API, and SDKs for integrating with mobile apps (e.g., Android SDK, iOS SDK)
Mobile app data modeling — designing data structures to store and process mobile app data, including schema design for NoSQL databases like MongoDB or Cassandra

Data processing pipelines for mobile app data — building scalable architectures using tools like Apache Beam, Apache Spark, or AWS Glue for transforming and loading mobile app data into a data warehouse or lake

Mobile app user behavior analysis — applying techniques from machine learning and statistical modeling to understand user behavior patterns and trends in mobile apps (e.g., sessionization, clustering)
Sensor data processing and integration — working with sensor data from mobile devices, including GPS, accelerometer, and gyroscope data, using tools like Apache Kafka or AWS Kinesis
Mobile app security considerations — understanding threats and vulnerabilities specific to mobile apps, such as data encryption, secure storage, and authentication mechanisms

knowledge.what_it_is: IoT refers to the network of physical devices, vehicles, buildings, and other items embedded with sensors, software, and connectivity that enables them to collect and exchange data with other devices and systems. As a Data Engineer, you will work with IoT data from various sources such as sensors, actuators, and gateways.
what_you_will_learn: IoT protocols — Understanding MQTT, CoAP, LWM2M, and HTTP/2 for device communication
Device management — Working with tools like AWS IoT Core, Google Cloud IoT Core, or Microsoft Azure IoT Hub to manage devices, monitor data, and perform actions
Data ingestion — Using libraries like Apache Kafka, Amazon Kinesis, or Google Cloud Pub/Sub to handle high-volume, high-velocity IoT data streams
Sensor data processing — Applying techniques like data normalization, filtering, and aggregation using tools like Apache Flink, Apache Spark, or AWS Lake Formation
Edge computing — Deploying compute resources at the edge of the network to reduce latency and improve real-time analytics with frameworks like EdgeX, OpenFog, or Fog Computing
Data storage — Designing and implementing data lakes or warehouses using tools like Amazon S3, Google Cloud Storage, or Apache HBase for IoT data
Security and authentication — Implementing secure communication protocols like TLS/SSL, DTLS, or IPsec to protect IoT device communications and data exchange

knowledge.what_it_is: Data normalization is the process of organizing data in a database to minimize data redundancy and dependency, improving data integrity and scalability. It involves dividing large tables into smaller ones based on relationships between entities.
what_you_will_learn: First Normal Form (1NF) — removing repeating groups by creating separate tables for each group
Second Normal Form (2NF) — ensuring that non-key attributes depend on the entire primary key, not just one part of it
Third Normal Form (3NF) — removing transitive dependencies by placing related data in a separate table
Boyce-Codd Normal Form (BCNF) — ensuring that there are no transitive dependencies and every determinant is a candidate key
Denormalization — intentionally denormalizing data for performance optimization, using techniques like materialized views or caching
Data Mart vs Data Warehouse — understanding the differences between these two related concepts in data warehousing
Star Schema vs Snowflake Schema — learning about the pros and cons of each schema design pattern in data warehousing

knowledge.what_it_is: Data modelling techniques are used by data engineers to design and implement efficient, scalable, and maintainable databases that meet business requirements.
what_you_will_learn: Entity-Relationship Modeling (ERM) — a conceptual model for database design using entities, attributes, and relationships
Object-Relational Mapping (ORM) — mapping objects to relational tables using frameworks like Hibernate or SQLAlchemy
Data Warehousing Design Patterns — designing star/snowflake schema for data warehousing and business intelligence applications
Dimensional Modeling — organizing data into facts and dimensions for efficient querying and analysis
Denormalization Techniques — storing redundant data in multiple forms to improve query performance
Materialized View Optimization — using materialized views to pre-compute complex queries and improve performance
subtopics: data modeling tools like erwin or powerdesigner

knowledge.what_it_is: OLTP (Online Transactional Processing) and OLAP (Online Analytical Processing) are two fundamental concepts in database design, used to optimize data storage and querying for transactional and analytical workloads respectively. In a Data Engineering role, understanding the differences between these two paradigms is crucial for designing efficient databases that meet business requirements.
what_you_will_learn: OLTP vs OLAP — key differences in database design for transactional vs analytical workloads
Normalization techniques — applying normalization rules to minimize data redundancy and improve data integrity (1NF, 2NF, 3NF)
Denormalization strategies — intentionally denormalizing tables for performance optimization in OLAP databases
Data warehousing concepts — designing a star or snowflake schema for efficient querying of large datasets
ETL (Extract Transform Load) processes — using tools like Apache Beam or AWS Glue to extract, transform and load data into a data warehouse
OLAP query optimization techniques — applying indexing strategies, caching, and materialized views to improve query performance
Data modeling for OLTP vs OLAP — understanding the trade-offs between data consistency, concurrency, and query performance

knowledge.what_it_is: SQL (Structured Query Language) is a standard language used to manage relational databases, allowing users to perform various operations such as creating, modifying, and querying data. As a Data Engineer, you will use SQL to design and optimize database schemas, write efficient queries, and ensure data consistency.
what_you_will_learn: SQL syntax basics — understanding the SELECT statement, FROM clause, WHERE clause, GROUP BY clause, HAVING clause, ORDER BY clause
Data types in SQL — learning about numeric, character, date/time, and binary data types, including their storage requirements and usage
Table relationships and joins — mastering INNER JOIN, LEFT JOIN, RIGHT JOIN, FULL OUTER JOIN, CROSS JOIN, and UNION operators to combine data from multiple tables
SQL functions and aggregations — understanding SUM, COUNT, AVG, MAX, MIN, GROUPING SETS, ROLLUP, CUBE, and window functions for data analysis
Indexing and optimization techniques — learning about B-tree indexes, hash indexes, clustered indexes, covering indexes, and query optimization strategies to improve performance
Database normalization and denormalization — understanding the importance of normal forms (1NF, 2NF, 3NF) and denormalizing data for specific use cases
SQL transactions and locking mechanisms — mastering COMMIT, ROLLBACK, SAVEPOINT statements, and row-level locking, table-level locking, and page-level locking
Common SQL commands — learning about CREATE TABLE, ALTER TABLE, DROP TABLE, TRUNCATE TABLE, INSERT INTO, UPDATE, DELETE, and MERGE statements

knowledge.what_it_is: Indexing is a database optimization technique that improves query performance by creating data structures (indexes) to speed up data retrieval and manipulation.
what_you_will_learn: B-Tree indexes — self-balancing search trees for efficient range queries, useful for large datasets
Hash indexes — fast lookup tables for exact matches, suitable for columns with unique values
Clustered indexes — storing index keys in the same order as data rows, reducing page splits and improving insert performance
Non-clustered indexes — separate storage of index keys from data rows, useful for large tables or infrequently updated columns
Index fragmentation — understanding how to monitor and manage fragmented indexes to prevent performance degradation
Index statistics — learning how to collect and maintain accurate statistics on index usage and cardinality
Covering indexes — creating indexes that include all necessary columns for a query, reducing the need for table scans
Composite indexes — combining multiple columns into a single index, useful for queries with complex WHERE clauses

knowledge.what_it_is: A transaction is a sequence of operations that are executed as a single, atomic unit to maintain data consistency and integrity in a database.
what_you_will_learn: ACID properties — Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability: ensuring transactions are processed reliably
Locking mechanisms — row-level locking, table-level locking, page-level locking: preventing concurrent modifications
Transaction isolation levels — Read Committed, Repeatable Read, Serializable: controlling concurrency and consistency
Two-Phase Commit (2PC) protocol — a distributed transaction protocol for ensuring atomicity across multiple resources
Pessimistic vs Optimistic Concurrency Control: understanding trade-offs between locking mechanisms and versioning
Transaction logging and recovery — techniques for maintaining database integrity in case of failures or crashes
Distributed transactions with NewSQL databases — handling complex transactions in modern, scalable systems

knowledge.what_it_is: MySQL is an open-source relational database management system (RDBMS) widely used by data engineers to store and manage structured data in a variety of industries, including e-commerce, finance, and healthcare.
what_you_will_learn: Database schema design — creating efficient and scalable table structures using MySQL's DDL statements (CREATE TABLE, ALTER TABLE)
SQL syntax basics — writing SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE queries to manipulate data in a database
Indexing strategies — optimizing query performance by creating indexes on columns used in WHERE, JOIN, and ORDER BY clauses
Database normalization — organizing data into tables to minimize data redundancy and improve data integrity using techniques like 1NF, 2NF, and 3NF
MySQL storage engines — understanding the differences between InnoDB and MyISAM storage engines and choosing the right one for a specific use case
Query optimization techniques — using EXPLAIN and SHOW INDEX to analyze query performance and optimize queries for better execution plans

knowledge.what_it_is: PostgreSQL is an open-source, object-relational database management system (ORDBMS) that supports both SQL and NoSQL data models. It is widely used in enterprise environments due to its reliability, scalability, and flexibility.
what_you_will_learn: Data Types — PostgreSQL supports various data types such as integers, strings, dates, timestamps, and JSON, which can be used to store and manipulate data efficiently
Table Structure — understanding how to create tables with primary keys, foreign keys, indexes, and constraints to maintain data integrity and optimize query performance
SQL Queries — writing efficient SQL queries using SELECT, FROM, WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING, and JOIN clauses to retrieve and manipulate data
Indexing — creating indexes on columns used in WHERE, JOIN, and ORDER BY clauses to improve query performance and reduce the load on the database
Transactions — understanding how to use transactions to ensure data consistency and isolation by using BEGIN, COMMIT, and ROLLBACK statements
Views — creating views to simplify complex queries and improve data security by hiding sensitive information from users
Stored Procedures — writing stored procedures to encapsulate business logic and improve code reusability
Security — configuring PostgreSQL to use SSL/TLS encryption, setting up user authentication and authorization, and using row-level security (RLS) to restrict access to sensitive data

knowledge.what_it_is: A Slowly Changing Dimension (SCD) is a data modeling technique used to manage changing attributes of dimension tables, ensuring data consistency and accuracy over time. It's commonly applied in data warehousing and business intelligence projects.
what_you_will_learn: Types of SCDs — Type I, II, III, IV: understanding the differences between them and when to use each
SCD Type I — full overwrite: replacing existing records with new ones, used for attributes that change frequently
SCD Type II — incremental update: storing historical changes in a separate table, used for attributes that change occasionally
SCD Type III — hybrid: combining Type I and Type II approaches, used for attributes with varying change frequencies
SCD Type IV — full overwrite with timestamp: similar to Type I but with additional timestamp column, used for auditing purposes
SCD implementation in data modeling tools — using SQL Server, Oracle, MySQL, PostgreSQL, or DB2
Data migration strategies — handling changes from previous SCD versions, including data cleansing and validation
Performance optimization techniques — indexing, partitioning, and caching to improve query performance on large SCD tables

knowledge.what_it_is: MariaDB is an open-source relational database management system (RDBMS) compatible with MySQL, developed by MariaDB Corporation. It's a drop-in replacement for MySQL and supports advanced features like Galera clustering and column-store storage.
what_you_will_learn: Database modeling — designing efficient schema using entities, attributes, and relationships
SQL syntax — writing queries using SELECT, FROM, WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING, JOIN, and subqueries
Indexing strategies — optimizing query performance with primary keys, foreign keys, indexes, and covering indexes
Query optimization — understanding EXPLAIN output to identify bottlenecks and improve query execution plans
Data types — working with integer, string, date, and timestamp data types in MariaDB
Stored procedures and functions — creating reusable code for complex database operations
Views — defining virtual tables based on queries to simplify data access and security
Backup and recovery strategies — using mysqldump, mariadb-backup, and xtrabackup for disaster recovery

knowledge.what_it_is: Amazon Aurora Database Service is a relational database service offered by AWS, designed to provide high performance, availability, and security for mission-critical applications. It supports MySQL and PostgreSQL workloads and offers automatic patching, backups, and scaling.
what_you_will_learn: Aurora DB clusters — creating and managing clusters with multiple instances, including primary and read replicas
Database engines — choosing between Aurora MySQL and PostgreSQL, understanding their differences and use cases
Storage types — selecting from SSD or HDD storage options, considering performance and cost trade-offs
High availability — configuring automatic failover, multi-AZ deployments, and read replica strategies for disaster recovery
Security features — implementing VPC security groups, IAM roles, and encryption at rest and in transit using AWS Key Management Service (KMS)
Performance optimization — tuning MySQL or PostgreSQL configuration parameters, leveraging query performance insights and monitoring tools like Amazon CloudWatch
Backup and restore — setting up automated backups, point-in-time recovery, and database snapshots for disaster recovery and data archiving

knowledge.what_it_is: Oracle is a commercial relational database management system (RDBMS) developed by Oracle Corporation, widely used in enterprise environments for its scalability, security, and high-performance capabilities. It supports various data types, including structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data.
what_you_will_learn: Database creation — creating a new database instance using SQL*Plus or Oracle Enterprise Manager
Tablespace management — organizing and managing storage for tables and indexes in Oracle
Indexing strategies — understanding when to use B-tree, bitmap, and function-based indexing
Partitioning techniques — dividing large tables into smaller, more manageable pieces for improved query performance
Materialized view refresh — automating the update of materialized views using Oracle's Materialized View Refresh feature
Oracle Data Guard — implementing high availability and disaster recovery using Oracle's built-in replication technology
SQL tuning — optimizing SQL queries to improve execution time and resource utilization

knowledge.what_it_is: Microsoft SQL Server (MS SQL) is a relational database management system developed by Microsoft, widely used in enterprise environments for storing and managing structured data. As a Data Engineer, you'll work with MS SQL to design, implement, and optimize databases for high-performance and scalability.
what_you_will_learn: Database Design — creating logical and physical database structures using entities, attributes, and relationships
Table Partitioning — dividing large tables into smaller, more manageable pieces based on specific criteria (e.g., date ranges)
Indexing Strategies — selecting the right indexing techniques (e.g., clustered, non-clustered, covering) to improve query performance
SQL Query Optimization — using tools like SQL Server Profiler and Query Store to identify bottlenecks and optimize queries for better execution plans
Backup and Recovery — implementing backup strategies (e.g., full, differential, transaction log) and restoring databases in case of failures or data loss
Database Security — configuring roles, permissions, and access control lists to ensure secure database operations
Data Types and Data Integrity — understanding MS SQL's data types (e.g., int, datetime, varchar) and implementing constraints (e.g., primary keys, foreign keys)
Stored Procedures and Functions — creating reusable code blocks for complex business logic and data processing

knowledge.what_it_is: A document database is a type of NoSQL database that stores data as self-describing documents, often in JSON or BSON format. It's designed to handle semi-structured and unstructured data, making it ideal for applications with varying schema requirements.
what_you_will_learn: Document structure — defining the layout and organization of documents within a collection
Data modeling — designing efficient document structures to store complex relationships between entities
Schema design — creating flexible schemas that accommodate changing data formats and requirements
Indexing strategies — optimizing query performance through proper indexing techniques, including single-field and compound indexes
Query patterns — understanding common query patterns in documents, such as filtering, sorting, and aggregation
Data validation — implementing data validation rules to ensure consistency and accuracy within documents
Document relationships — modeling relationships between documents using techniques like referencing and embedding
Data denormalization — storing redundant data to improve query performance and reduce latency
Caching strategies — optimizing document retrieval by employing caching mechanisms, such as Redis or Memcached
Security considerations — implementing access control and authentication mechanisms to protect sensitive documents

knowledge.what_it_is: MongoDB is a popular NoSQL document-based database, known for its high scalability and flexibility. It's widely used in modern data engineering pipelines due to its ability to handle large amounts of semi-structured data.
what_you_will_learn: Data modeling — designing MongoDB collections and documents to optimize data retrieval and storage
Querying with MongoDB Query Language (MQL) — writing efficient queries using the $and, $or, and $not operators
Indexing strategies — creating indexes on specific fields to improve query performance
Aggregation framework — using the pipeline-based aggregation framework for complex data processing and analysis
Change Streams — monitoring changes to MongoDB collections in real-time using the Change Stream API
GridFS — storing large files in MongoDB using GridFS, a file system for storing binary data
Replication and sharding — designing and implementing high-availability and scalability solutions using MongoDB's built-in replication and sharding features
subtopics: mongodb data modeling patterns (denormalization, data warehousing)

knowledge.what_it_is: CouchDB is a NoSQL, document-oriented database that stores data as JSON documents, allowing for flexible schema design and high scalability. It is designed to handle large amounts of unstructured or semi-structured data and provides a RESTful API for data access.
what_you_will_learn: Document structure — understanding how CouchDB stores data as JSON documents with fields, keys, and values
Views — creating custom indexes on specific document fields using MapReduce functions for efficient querying
Replication — setting up automatic data replication between nodes for high availability and disaster recovery
Sharding — distributing data across multiple servers to improve performance and scalability
CouchDB API — understanding the RESTful API for CRUD operations, including GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE requests
Document updates — handling changes to existing documents using the _rev field for conflict resolution
Conflict resolution — resolving document conflicts that occur during concurrent updates or replication
Security — configuring user authentication and authorization using CouchDB's built-in security features

knowledge.what_it_is: A key-value store is a type of NoSQL database that stores data as a collection of key-value pairs, where each key uniquely identifies a value. In the context of Data Engineering, key-value stores are used for caching, session management, and other use cases where fast lookup and insertion of data is required.
what_you_will_learn: Key-Value Store Architecture — understanding the basic components of a key-value store, including storage nodes, cache layers, and consistency models
Data Modeling for Key-Value Stores — designing efficient data structures to store and retrieve data in key-value stores, including techniques like sharding and partitioning
Cache Hierarchy Design — implementing cache hierarchies to optimize performance and reduce latency in key-value store applications
Consistency Models — understanding the trade-offs between consistency models like strong consistency, eventual consistency, and relaxed consistency in key-value stores
Data Replication Strategies — designing data replication strategies for high availability and durability in key-value store applications
Key-Value Store Implementations — comparing and contrasting popular key-value store implementations like Redis, Memcached, Riak, and Amazon DynamoDB
Querying Key-Value Stores — understanding how to query key-value stores using APIs like HLL (HyperLogLog) for cardinality estimation and other specialized data structures
Performance Optimization Techniques — applying techniques like caching, compression, and data deduplication to optimize performance in key-value store applications
Security Considerations — understanding security considerations when implementing key-value stores, including authentication, authorization, and encryption

knowledge.what_it_is: Redis is an open-source, in-memory data store that can be used as a database, message broker, or caching layer. It supports various data structures such as strings, hashes, lists, sets, and maps.
what_you_will_learn: Data modeling with Redis — designing efficient data structures for storing and retrieving data
Redis keys and values — understanding the basics of key-value pairs, expiration times, and TTLs (time-to-lives)
Redis data types — mastering strings, hashes, lists, sets, maps, and other advanced data types
Redis transactions — using MULTI/EXEC commands for atomic operations and minimizing data inconsistencies
Redis pub/sub messaging — implementing message queues and event-driven architectures with Redis
Redis clustering — scaling Redis horizontally across multiple nodes for high availability and performance
Redis persistence — configuring AOF (append-only file) and RDB (RDBMS dump) for data durability and recovery

knowledge.what_it_is: Memcached is an open-source, high-performance caching system that stores data in RAM to reduce the load on databases and improve application responsiveness.
what_you_will_learn: Caching fundamentals — understanding how Memcached works, its advantages, and use cases
Data serialization formats — learning about supported formats like text, binary, and JSON
Key-value store architecture — understanding how Memcached stores data as key-value pairs in RAM
Cache expiration strategies — implementing techniques like time-to-live (TTL), least recently used (LRU), and most recently used (MRU)
Memcached configuration options — learning about settings like max connections, timeout, and eviction policy
Data retrieval and storage — understanding how to store and retrieve data from Memcached using APIs like get, set, delete, and incr
Connection pooling and load balancing — implementing strategies for efficient connection management and load distribution
Integration with databases and applications — learning about common integration patterns and tools like memcached-python-client

knowledge.what_it_is: DynamoDB is a fast, fully managed NoSQL database service offered by Amazon Web Services (AWS), designed to handle large amounts of data across multiple applications and services. It provides high performance, scalability, and reliability for big data workloads.
what_you_will_learn: Table design — creating optimized table structures with primary keys, secondary indexes, and sort keys
Data modeling — designing efficient data models using DynamoDB's attribute data types, including strings, numbers, and binary data
Querying — writing efficient queries using DynamoDB's query language, including filtering, sorting, and pagination
Scanning — reading large amounts of data from a table using DynamoDB's scan operation, with considerations for performance and cost
Transactions — handling concurrent updates to multiple items in a single transaction using DynamoDB's transaction API
Streams and triggers — setting up event-driven architectures using DynamoDB Streams and AWS Lambda triggers
Backup and recovery — configuring automatic backups and point-in-time recovery for high availability and disaster recovery

knowledge.what_it_is: Cosmos DB is a globally distributed, multi-model database service provided by Microsoft Azure. It supports document, key-value, and graph data models, and provides high availability, scalability, and performance.
what_you_will_learn: DocumentDB — storing semi-structured JSON documents with flexible schema
Table API — working with structured data in a table format, similar to relational databases
Gremlin API — querying graph data using the Gremlin query language
Change Feed — tracking changes made to documents and tables for real-time analytics
Stored Procedures — executing server-side logic on Cosmos DB data
Triggers — automating tasks based on changes to documents and tables
Data Partitioning — distributing data across multiple partitions for high availability and performance
Consistency Models — choosing between strong, eventual, or session consistency models
subtopics: cosmos db security and access control

knowledge.what_it_is: NoSQL databases are designed to handle large amounts of unstructured or semi-structured data, providing flexible schema designs and high scalability. They are commonly used in big data and real-time web applications.
what_you_will_learn: Document-based databases — storing data as JSON-like documents, e.g., MongoDB, Couchbase
Key-value stores — storing data as key-value pairs, e.g., Riak, Redis
Column-family databases — storing data in columns instead of rows, e.g., Cassandra, HBase
Graph databases — storing data as graphs, e.g., Neo4j, Amazon Neptune
Data modeling for NoSQL databases — designing schema-less or schema-defined models for efficient querying and storage
Sharding and partitioning strategies — distributing data across multiple nodes for horizontal scaling
Consistency models — understanding eventual consistency, strong consistency, and other trade-offs in distributed systems
CAP theorem implications — understanding the consequences of choosing between consistency, availability, and partition tolerance

knowledge.what_it_is: ElasticSearch is a popular, open-source, distributed search and analytics engine developed by Elastic (formerly known as Elasticsearch N.V.). It's widely used in data engineering roles for its scalability, flexibility, and ease of use. As a Data Engineer, you'll work with ElasticSearch to build efficient search applications, log analysis pipelines, and real-time analytics systems.
what_you_will_learn: Indexing — creating and managing indexes for storing and querying data
Mappings — defining the structure of documents in an index using JSON-like syntax
Types — understanding the difference between document types and how to use them ( deprecated since ES7.0)
Queries — building complex queries using Lucene query DSL, including filtering, sorting, and aggregations
Mappings with dynamic templates — defining reusable mapping templates for efficient data modeling
ElasticSearch API clients — working with official clients in languages like Java, Python, or Node.js to interact with the ElasticSearch cluster
Ingesting data into ElasticSearch using Logstash, Fluentd, or Beats
Configuring and managing ElasticSearch nodes, including clustering, replication, and shard allocation

knowledge.what_it_is: Column storage and retrieval is a fundamental concept in NoSQL databases, where data is stored in tables with multiple columns, allowing for efficient querying and indexing. It's essential for Data Engineers to understand columnar storage, its advantages over traditional row-based storage, and how it impacts query performance.
what_you_will_learn: Column families — organizing related columns together for efficient storage and retrieval
Column keys — unique identifiers for each column, used in querying and indexing
Data compression — techniques like Run-Length Encoding (RLE) and Delta encoding to reduce storage needs
Encoding schemes — understanding how different encodings (e.g., UTF-8, ASCII) impact data size and query performance
Column-level security — controlling access to specific columns based on user permissions or roles
Materialized views — pre-computed results of complex queries stored in a columnar format for faster querying
Column-oriented storage engines — understanding the trade-offs between column-stores (e.g., Apache Cassandra, Amazon Redshift) and row-stores (e.g., MySQL, PostgreSQL)
Data partitioning — strategies for dividing large datasets into smaller, more manageable pieces across multiple columns
Indexing techniques — using B-trees, hash tables, or other indexing methods to speed up query performance in columnar storage

knowledge.what_it_is: Apache Cassandra is a free and open-source, distributed, NoSQL database management system designed to handle large amounts of data across many commodity servers with minimal latency. It is highly scalable, fault-tolerant, and provides high availability.
what_you_will_learn: Data modeling — designing Cassandra tables for efficient data retrieval and storage
CQL (Cassandra Query Language) — writing SQL-like queries to interact with Cassandra databases
Data partitioning — understanding how Cassandra splits data across multiple nodes for improved performance
Replication strategies — configuring replication factors, read repairs, and anti-entropy repair for high availability
Tunable throughput and latency (TTL) — setting time-to-live values for data expiration and garbage collection
Materialized views — creating pre-computed results of queries for faster data retrieval
Cassandra drivers — using Java, Python, or other language-specific drivers to connect to Cassandra databases

knowledge.what_it_is: HBase is an open-source, distributed, NoSQL database built on top of Hadoop's HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System), designed to store and manage large amounts of semi-structured data. It provides a flexible schema and supports high-performance random access and real-time queries.
what_you_will_learn: Table structure — defining table names, column families, and column qualifiers
Data model — understanding HBase's key-value data model and its implications for data storage and retrieval
Row key design — designing efficient row keys for fast lookup and retrieval of data
Column family design — organizing related columns into families to improve query performance
HBase API — using the Java Client API or Thrift API to interact with HBase tables
Scan and filter — understanding how to efficiently scan and filter large datasets in HBase
Put, Get, Delete operations — mastering basic CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) operations on HBase tables

knowledge.what_it_is: Neo4j is a graph database that uses Cypher query language and supports ACID transactions, suitable for complex data relationships and network analysis.
what_you_will_learn: Cypher Query Language — writing queries to traverse and manipulate graph data
Node Properties — storing key-value pairs on nodes in the graph
Relationships — modeling connections between nodes with labels and properties
Graph Schema Design — designing efficient graph structures for querying and indexing
Neo4j Drivers — using official drivers for Java, Python, .NET, and other languages to interact with Neo4j
Cypher Functions — using built-in functions for aggregation, filtering, and data manipulation
Indexing Strategies — optimizing query performance with node and relationship indexes

knowledge.what_it_is: Neptune is a fully-managed, distributed, and scalable graph database service offered by Amazon Web Services (AWS). It provides a highly available and durable storage solution for large-scale graph data, enabling efficient querying and analysis of complex relationships between entities.
what_you_will_learn: Graph Data Model — understanding the concept of nodes, edges, and properties in Neptune
Schema Design — designing optimal schema for efficient query performance and data retrieval
Querying — writing Cypher queries to traverse and retrieve graph data from Neptune
Indexing — creating and managing indexes on properties for improved query performance
Data Ingestion — loading data into Neptune using AWS Glue, AWS Lambda, or Amazon Kinesis
Data Retrieval — retrieving data from Neptune using AWS SDKs or AWS CLI
Security and Access Control — configuring IAM roles and permissions to access Neptune resources

knowledge.what_it_is: Bigtable is a fully managed, petabyte-scale NoSQL database service offered by Google Cloud. It provides high-performance and low-latency data storage and retrieval capabilities for large datasets.
what_you_will_learn: Column Families — organizing related columns together to improve query performance
Row Keys — unique identifiers for each row in Bigtable, used for fast lookup and retrieval
Cell Values — storing and retrieving data as key-value pairs within cells
Timestamps — versioning cell values over time using timestamps
Bigtable Client Libraries — using client libraries like Java, Python, or Go to interact with Bigtable
Cloud Bigtable Admin API — managing Bigtable instances, tables, and clusters through the Cloud Console or CLI
Data Ingestion — loading data into Bigtable from various sources like Apache Beam, Apache Spark, or Google Cloud Storage
Data Retrieval — querying and retrieving data from Bigtable using SQL-like syntax with Cloud Bigtable SQL

knowledge.what_it_is: A data warehouse is a centralized, subject-oriented, integrated, time-variant, and non-volatile repository of data that supports business intelligence activities. It's designed to provide a single source of truth for decision-making, combining data from various sources into a unified view.
what_you_will_learn: Data warehousing concepts — understanding the purpose, benefits, and challenges of implementing a data warehouse
ETL/ELT processes — learning about extract-transform-load (ETL) and extract-load-transform (ELT) methodologies for data integration
Star/Snowflake schema design — designing efficient data structures to support fast querying and analytics
Data modeling techniques — understanding how to create logical and physical data models for a data warehouse
Dimensional modeling — learning about the process of creating dimensions, facts, and measures in a data warehouse
Data governance and security — understanding how to implement access control, data encryption, and auditing in a data warehouse
Data quality and cleansing — learning about techniques for ensuring data accuracy, completeness, and consistency
Data warehousing tools and technologies — familiarizing yourself with popular data warehousing platforms like AWS Redshift, Google BigQuery, Snowflake, and Azure Synapse Analytics

knowledge.what_it_is: A data warehousing architecture is a design pattern that enables the integration of data from various sources into a centralized repository, supporting business intelligence and analytics. In the context of a Data Engineer role, it involves designing and implementing scalable, secure, and performant data warehouses to meet organizational needs.
what_you_will_learn: Star Schema — a widely used data warehousing architecture that organizes data in a star-shaped structure, with facts at the center and dimensions radiating outwards
Snowflake Schema — an extension of the Star Schema, allowing for multiple levels of granularity and improved query performance
Fact Table — a central table containing aggregated data, often used as the fact table in a star schema
Dimension Table — a table containing descriptive attributes about the facts, such as date, time, or location
ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) Process — a common approach for integrating data from various sources into a data warehouse
Data Ingestion Tools like Apache NiFi, AWS Glue, or Google Cloud Dataflow — used to extract and load data into the data warehouse
Data Modeling Techniques like Kimball's Dimensional Modeling or Inmon's Enterprise Data Warehouse — used to design and implement data warehouses
subtopics: column-store databases like amazon redshift or google bigquery
row-store databases like postgresql or mysql

knowledge.what_it_is: A Star schema and Snowflake schema are two types of data warehouse architectures used to optimize query performance, scalability, and data modeling. The main difference between them lies in their dimensionality and how they handle large amounts of data.
what_you_will_learn: Star Schema — a simple, fact-centric design with dimensions as separate tables, ideal for OLAP (Online Analytical Processing) workloads
Snowflake Schema — a more complex, multi-dimensional design that combines aspects of both star and fact constellation schemas, suitable for large-scale data warehousing and big data analytics
Fact Tables — central tables containing measures or metrics, often denormalized to improve query performance
Dimension Tables — separate tables containing descriptive attributes about the facts, used to filter and aggregate data
Star Join — a join operation that combines fact and dimension tables, optimized for fast querying and aggregations
Snowflake Join — an extension of star joins, allowing for more complex queries and multiple levels of aggregation
Data Warehousing Design Patterns — understanding when to use each schema type, including considerations for data volume, velocity, and variety
ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) Process Optimization — strategies for loading data into a Star or Snowflake schema, including data cleansing, transformation, and loading techniques

knowledge.what_it_is: A data warehouse is a centralized repository that stores and manages data from various sources, providing business intelligence and analytics capabilities to organizations. As a Data Engineer, you will design, implement, and maintain data warehouses using ETL tools, databases, and cloud platforms.
what_you_will_learn: Data Warehouse Architecture — designing star/snowflake schema, understanding dimensional modeling
ETL Tools — using Apache NiFi, Talend, Informatica PowerCenter for data extraction, transformation, and loading
Data Modeling — creating conceptual, logical, and physical data models using tools like ERwin, IBM InfoSphere Data Architect
Database Selection — choosing between relational databases (Oracle, PostgreSQL), NoSQL databases (MongoDB, Cassandra), or cloud-native databases (Amazon Redshift, Google BigQuery)
Cloud Platforms — designing and deploying data warehouses on AWS (Redshift, Glue), Azure (Synapse Analytics), GCP (BigQuery, Dataflow)
Data Ingestion — using Apache Beam, Spark, or Flink for batch/real-time data processing and ingestion
Data Quality and Governance — implementing data validation, data lineage, and metadata management using tools like Talend, Informatica
Security and Access Control — designing secure data warehouses with role-based access control, encryption, and authentication mechanisms

knowledge.what_it_is: A Data Mart is a subset of data from a Data Warehouse, optimized for querying and analysis by specific business units or departments. It's a centralized repository of data that supports decision-making and reporting.
what_you_will_learn: Data Mart Design Patterns — designing efficient and scalable Data Marts using star/snowflake schema, fact tables, and dimension tables
ETL Process for Data Mart Loading — understanding the Extract, Transform, Load (ETL) process for populating a Data Mart with data from various sources
Data Modeling for Data Marts — creating conceptual, logical, and physical data models to support Data Mart design and development
Data Warehouse vs. Data Mart — understanding the key differences between a Data Warehouse and a Data Mart, including their purposes, benefits, and use cases
Data Mart Security and Access Control — implementing security measures such as row-level security, data masking, and access control lists to protect sensitive data in a Data Mart
Data Mart Maintenance and Refresh — understanding the importance of regularly updating and refreshing a Data Mart with new data to ensure its accuracy and relevance
Data Mart Performance Tuning — optimizing query performance in a Data Mart by indexing, caching, and partitioning data
Data Mart Governance and Metadata Management — establishing governance policies and metadata management practices to ensure data quality, consistency, and compliance in a Data Mart

knowledge.what_it_is: A data fabric is a unified, enterprise-wide data management platform that enables seamless integration and governance of diverse data sources, formats, and systems. It provides a single pane of glass for data discovery, cataloging, and lineage, ensuring data consistency, quality, and security across the organization.
what_you_will_learn: Data Fabric Architecture — designing a scalable, distributed architecture for data integration and governance
Data Lineage — tracking data provenance, including source systems, transformations, and storage locations
Metadata Management — storing, managing, and versioning metadata across the data fabric
Data Governance Frameworks — implementing policies, roles, and access controls for data security and compliance
Data Quality Metrics — defining and monitoring metrics for data accuracy, completeness, and consistency
Data Integration Patterns — applying patterns like ETL, ELT, CDC, and change data capture for data synchronization
Cloud-Native Data Fabrics — designing data fabrics that leverage cloud-native services like AWS Lake Formation, GCP BigQuery, or Azure Synapse Analytics
Real-Time Data Processing — integrating real-time data processing engines like Apache Flink, Apache Spark Streaming, or Amazon Kinesis

knowledge.what_it_is: A Data Hub is a centralized platform that enables data integration, governance, and sharing across an organization. It provides a single source of truth for all data assets, ensuring consistency, quality, and security.
what_you_will_learn: Data Ingestion — designing pipelines to collect data from various sources using Apache NiFi, AWS Kinesis, or Confluent Kafka
Data Transformation — applying ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) processes using tools like AWS Glue, Apache Beam, or Talend
Data Storage — selecting suitable storage solutions such as Amazon S3, Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2, or Google Cloud Storage for data at rest
Data Cataloging — implementing metadata management using tools like AWS Lake Formation, Apache Atlas, or Collibra to ensure data discoverability and governance
Data Access Control — designing access control mechanisms using techniques like row-level security (RLS) or attribute-based access control (ABAC)
Data Lineage — tracking data provenance and lineage using tools like AWS X-Ray, Apache Airflow, or Datafold to ensure transparency and accountability

knowledge.what_it_is: A metadata-first architecture is an approach to data management where metadata, or data about data, is given priority over raw data itself. This allows for better data governance, improved data quality, and more efficient querying and processing of data.
what_you_will_learn: Metadata modeling — creating a structured representation of metadata using entities, attributes, and relationships
Data cataloging — building a centralized repository of metadata to provide context and meaning to raw data
Tagging and classification — assigning relevant tags and classifications to metadata to enable search and filtering
Data lineage tracking — maintaining a record of the origin, processing history, and transformations applied to each dataset
Metadata-driven data governance — using metadata to enforce policies, ensure compliance, and manage access control
Data quality monitoring — leveraging metadata to track data accuracy, completeness, and consistency
Self-service analytics — enabling business users to query and analyze data through a user-friendly interface
API-first design — designing APIs that expose metadata and data in a standardized, machine-readable format
subtopics: data lineage tools (e.g. apache nifi, aws glue)
self-service analytics platforms (e.g. tableau, power bi)
validation_keywords: data lineage tools (e.g. apache nifi, aws glue)

knowledge.what_it_is: Serverless computing allows developers to build and deploy applications without managing servers, using cloud providers' resources on-demand.
what_you_will_learn: AWS Lambda — a serverless compute service that executes code only when triggered by events
Azure Functions — a fully managed event-driven serverless compute service for building scalable applications
Google Cloud Functions — a serverless compute service that allows developers to write and deploy small code snippets in response to cloud events
Cloud Events — a vendor-agnostic standard for defining event structures, enabling seamless integration between cloud services
Function as a Service (FaaS) — a programming model where the provider manages the underlying infrastructure and runtime environment
Event-driven architecture — designing systems that respond to events triggered by users or external sources
Serverless frameworks — tools like AWS SAM, Azure Functions Core Tools, and Google Cloud SDK that simplify serverless development and deployment

knowledge.what_it_is: Databricks Delta Lake is an open-source, scalable, and highly available storage layer for data lakes, optimized for Apache Spark. It provides ACID transactions, data versioning, and schema-on-read capabilities.
what_you_will_learn: Delta Lake architecture — understanding the components of a Databricks Delta Lake ecosystem, including the metastore, table format, and storage
Creating Delta tables — using the `CREATE TABLE` statement to create a new Delta table with specified properties, such as partitioning and compression
Inserting data into Delta tables — using the `INSERT INTO` statement or Spark DataFrames to insert data into an existing Delta table
Updating and deleting data in Delta tables — using transactions and ACID compliance to ensure data consistency and integrity
Optimizing Delta Lake performance — understanding how to configure partitioning, caching, and other settings to improve query performance
Delta Lake schema evolution — managing changes to the schema of a Delta table over time, including adding or removing columns
Data versioning in Delta Lake — using the `SNAPSHOT` feature to track changes to data over time and manage different versions of data
