knowledge.what_it_is: Streamlit is an open-source Python library used by data engineers to create web applications and dashboards, allowing users to easily integrate machine learning models, data visualizations, and other components into a single interface.
what_you_will_learn: Creating Streamlit apps using the `streamlit run` command or embedding in Jupyter notebooks
Using Streamlit's core components: `st.title`, `st.header`, `st.subheader`, `st.markdown`, and `st.text` for layout and styling
Integrating machine learning models with Streamlit using scikit-learn, TensorFlow, PyTorch, or other libraries
Creating interactive visualizations with Plotly, Matplotlib, Seaborn, or Bokeh within a Streamlit app
Using Streamlit's caching mechanism to speed up computation and improve performance
Implementing authentication and authorization in Streamlit apps using OAuth, JWT, or other methods
Deploying Streamlit apps on cloud platforms like AWS, Google Cloud, Azure, or Heroku

knowledge.what_it_is: Tableau is a data visualization and business intelligence tool that connects to various data sources, allowing users to create interactive dashboards, reports, and stories. It is widely used by data engineers to provide data-driven insights to stakeholders.
what_you_will_learn: Connecting to data sources — Tableau can connect to relational databases (e.g., PostgreSQL), cloud storage services (e.g., AWS S3), and big data platforms (e.g., Hadoop, Spark)
Data modeling — creating logical data models in Tableau to represent complex relationships between tables and fields
Dimensional modeling — designing dimensions and measures for efficient data analysis and visualization
Table calculations — using formulas and functions to perform calculations on data within Tableau
Set analysis — combining multiple datasets or tables to create a single, unified view of the data
Data blending — merging data from different sources into a single dataset for analysis
Storytelling with dashboards — creating interactive dashboards that tell a story with data and provide insights to stakeholders

knowledge.what_it_is: Looker is an enterprise data platform that enables data engineers to build and maintain data models, connect to various data sources, and create interactive dashboards. It provides a SQL interface for querying data and supports real-time analytics.
what_you_will_learn: Data modeling — creating logical data models using LookML (Looker's proprietary modeling language) to define data relationships and structures
Data connections — establishing connections to various data sources such as relational databases, cloud storage services, and big data platforms like Hadoop and Spark
Dashboard creation — building interactive dashboards using Looker's UI components and visualizations to present insights to stakeholders
SQL querying — writing SQL queries to extract data from connected sources and perform complex analytics
Data security and governance — implementing access controls, row-level security, and data encryption to ensure data integrity and compliance
LookML functions and filters — using Looker's proprietary functions and filters to manipulate data and create custom calculations
Dashboard embedding — integrating Looker dashboards into web applications using APIs and SDKs

knowledge.what_it_is: Metadata management is the process of collecting, storing, and maintaining data that describes other data in an organization's data assets. It involves creating a centralized repository to store metadata, which can include information about data sources, formats, quality, security, and usage.
what_you_will_learn: Data cataloging — creating a centralized repository to store metadata, including data sources, formats, quality, security, and usage
Metadata modeling — designing a metadata schema to capture relevant information about data assets
Data lineage tracking — recording the origin, processing history, and dependencies of data flows
Data governance policies — establishing rules and standards for metadata management, access control, and data quality
Metadata integration with data warehouses and lakes — loading metadata into data warehousing and lake environments for analytics and reporting
Automated metadata discovery — using tools like Apache Atlas or AWS Lake Formation to automatically discover and catalog metadata
Metadata standardization — defining common metadata standards across the organization to ensure consistency and interoperability
subtopics: metadata integration with data warehouses and lakes
validation_keywords: metadata integration with data warehouses and lakes

knowledge.what_it_is: Data interoperability refers to the ability of different systems, applications, and data formats to exchange and use data seamlessly, without loss of meaning or context. As a Data Engineer, you will ensure that data is accessible, usable, and consistent across various platforms and tools.
what_you_will_learn: API design patterns — designing APIs for maximum flexibility and reusability (e.g., RESTful API, GraphQL)
Data format standards — understanding and implementing industry-standard formats like CSV, JSON, Avro, Parquet
Schema-on-Read vs. Schema-on-Write — choosing the right approach for data processing and storage
Data mapping and transformation — using tools like Apache Beam, AWS Glue, or Talend to transform data between formats
API gateways and proxies — using tools like NGINX, Amazon API Gateway, or Google Cloud Endpoints to manage API interactions
Data virtualization — abstracting complex data sources into simple, unified interfaces (e.g., Apache Atlas, AWS Lake Formation)
Event-driven architecture — designing systems that respond to real-time events and notifications (e.g., Apache Kafka, Amazon Kinesis)
Service-oriented architecture — breaking down monolithic applications into reusable services (e.g., microservices)

knowledge.what_it_is: Data quality refers to the accuracy, completeness, and consistency of data within an organization's systems and processes. As a Data Engineer, ensuring high-quality data is crucial for making informed business decisions and driving operational efficiency.
what_you_will_learn: Data profiling — creating detailed summaries of data distributions, including mean, median, mode, and standard deviation
Data validation rules — defining and enforcing constraints on data formats, such as date ranges, phone number patterns, or email addresses
Data normalization — transforming raw data into a consistent format for analysis and reporting, including handling missing values and outliers
Data cleansing — identifying and correcting errors in data, including duplicate detection, data deduplication, and data standardization
Data lineage — tracking the origin, processing history, and dependencies of data to ensure transparency and accountability
Data quality metrics — defining and monitoring key performance indicators (KPIs) for data quality, such as accuracy, completeness, and consistency
Data governance frameworks — implementing policies and procedures for data management, including roles, responsibilities, and access controls

knowledge.what_it_is: Data lineage refers to the process of tracking and documenting the origin, processing history, and movement of data throughout its lifecycle within an organization's data ecosystem. It involves understanding how data is created, transformed, and consumed across various systems, applications, and teams.
what_you_will_learn: Data Lineage Frameworks — implementing frameworks like Apache Atlas, AWS Lake Formation, or Google Cloud Data Catalog to manage metadata and track data lineage
Data Flow Graphs — visualizing data flows using tools like Apache Beam, Apache Spark, or AWS Glue to understand data processing pipelines
Metadata Management — storing and managing metadata in repositories like Apache Hive, Apache Cassandra, or Amazon S3 to support data governance and auditing
Data Provenance — tracking the origin and history of data using techniques like digital watermarking, data hashing, or blockchain-based solutions
Change Data Capture (CDC) — implementing CDC mechanisms like Debezium, AWS DMS, or Oracle GoldenGate to capture changes in source systems and propagate them to target systems
Data Quality Metrics — defining and tracking metrics like data accuracy, completeness, and consistency using tools like Apache NiFi, Apache Airflow, or Informatica PowerCenter
Audit Trails — creating audit trails to record all data access, modification, and deletion activities using tools like Apache Ranger, AWS IAM, or Google Cloud Audit Logs

knowledge.what_it_is: The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) is a European Union regulation that sets out to protect and respect individuals' rights over their personal data. As a Data Engineer, you will be responsible for ensuring that your organization complies with GDPR regulations when processing and storing sensitive data.
what_you_will_learn: Data Subject Rights — understanding the rights of individuals under GDPR, including right to access, rectification, erasure, restriction of processing, data portability, and objection
Personal Data Categories — identifying and classifying personal data types (e.g., name, email, address, IP address) and ensuring they are handled accordingly
Data Minimization Principle — implementing measures to minimize the collection and storage of personal data, adhering to the principle of least privilege
Consent Management — designing systems for obtaining, recording, and managing consent from individuals for processing their personal data
Data Protection by Design and Default (PbD) — incorporating data protection principles into system design and default settings to ensure GDPR compliance
Data Breach Notification — understanding the requirements for notifying affected parties in case of a data breach and implementing incident response plans
GDPR Compliance Frameworks — familiarizing yourself with frameworks like ISO 27001, NIST Cybersecurity Framework, or SOC 2 that support GDPR compliance
Data Anonymization and Pseudonymization — techniques for masking personal data to reduce risk while still allowing analysis and processing

knowledge.what_it_is: The EU AI Act is a regulatory framework aimed at ensuring the safe and transparent development of artificial intelligence (AI) systems within the European Union. As a Data Engineer, it's essential to understand the implications of this regulation on your work, particularly in data governance, model explainability, and bias mitigation.
what_you_will_learn: Data protection by design — designing AI systems with built-in data protection mechanisms to ensure compliance
Transparency requirements for AI decision-making processes — understanding how to provide clear explanations of AI-driven decisions
Bias mitigation techniques — implementing strategies to detect and mitigate bias in AI models, including data preprocessing and regularization
Explainability methods for complex AI systems — applying techniques like SHAP values, LIME, or saliency maps to explain AI-driven decisions
Data governance frameworks for AI development — understanding how to implement data governance policies and procedures within AI projects
Risk assessment and mitigation strategies for AI deployment — identifying potential risks and developing mitigation plans for AI systems
Compliance with EU AI Act regulations — understanding the specific requirements of the EU AI Act, including data protection, transparency, and accountability
subtopics: explainability methods for complex ai systems
data governance frameworks for ai development
ai risk assessment and mitigation strategies
validation_keywords: explainability methods for complex ai systems

knowledge.what_it_is: The European Union's General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and the ePrivacy Directive, also known as the Electronic Communications Privacy Act (ECPA), regulate data protection and privacy for individuals within the EU. As a Data Engineer, understanding ECPA is crucial to ensure compliance with data processing regulations and protect sensitive information.
what_you_will_learn: Data subject rights — understanding individual's rights to access, rectify, erase, restrict, or object to their personal data
Consent management — obtaining, storing, and managing consent for data processing, including explicit and implicit consent
Data minimization — implementing measures to collect only necessary and relevant data for the intended purpose
Pseudonymization — techniques for masking sensitive information, such as encryption or tokenization
Data protection by design and default — designing systems with data protection in mind from the outset
Data breach notification — procedures for detecting, reporting, and responding to data breaches
Data portability — enabling individuals to transfer their personal data between services or platforms
Right to erasure (right to be forgotten) — implementing measures to erase personal data upon request
subtopics: data protection by design and default

knowledge.what_it_is: ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) and Reverse ETL are data integration patterns used to move data between systems, with ETL focusing on loading data into a destination system and Reverse ETL focusing on extracting transformed data from a warehouse or lake back into operational systems.
what_you_will_learn: ETL pipeline architecture — designing pipelines for efficient data movement and transformation
Data warehousing vs data lakes — understanding the differences in storing and processing data
ELT (Extract, Load, Transform) vs ETL — comparing the two patterns and their use cases
Reverse ETL tools like Hightouch, Census, Segment — understanding their features and benefits
ETL vs Reverse ETL trade-offs — weighing the pros and cons of each approach for different use cases
Data quality and governance in ETL and Reverse ETL pipelines — ensuring data accuracy and compliance
Real-time data integration using event-driven architectures — integrating ETL and Reverse ETL with streaming data

knowledge.what_it_is: Reverse ETL is a process of extracting data from a data warehouse or lake and loading it into operational systems, such as CRM, marketing automation platforms, or customer support tools. It's used to keep these systems up-to-date with the latest customer data, enabling real-time personalization and improved customer experiences.
what_you_will_learn: Data synchronization — synchronizing customer data across multiple systems in real-time
Event-driven architecture — using events to trigger data updates in operational systems
Change data capture (CDC) — capturing changes made to data in the warehouse or lake and applying them to operational systems
Delta Lake — using Delta Lake's incremental update capabilities for efficient reverse ETL processing
Apache Beam — using Apache Beam's unified programming model for building scalable reverse ETL pipelines
Fivetran — using Fivetran's cloud-based platform for automating reverse ETL workflows
Matillion — using Matillion's data integration and automation platform for reverse ETL tasks

knowledge.what_it_is: Hightouch is a Reverse ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) tool that enables data engineers to integrate customer data from various sources into marketing and analytics platforms. It provides a user-friendly interface for defining transformations and mappings between source and destination systems.
what_you_will_learn: Reverse ETL concepts — understanding the need for Reverse ETL in modern data engineering, how it differs from traditional ETL
Hightouch Data Model — designing and implementing custom data models to integrate with various marketing and analytics platforms
Data Transformations — defining and applying transformations using Hightouch's SQL-like query language to prepare data for loading
Destination Integrations — connecting Hightouch to popular marketing and analytics platforms such as Segment, Google Analytics, and Mixpanel
Data Quality and Validation — implementing data quality checks and validation rules to ensure accurate and reliable data integration
Hightouch APIs and SDKs — using Hightouch's APIs and SDKs to automate data integration processes and build custom workflows
Monitoring and Debugging — setting up monitoring and debugging tools to troubleshoot issues and optimize data integration performance

knowledge.what_it_is: Census is the process of collecting and aggregating data from various sources to create a comprehensive view of an organization's or population's characteristics, demographics, and behaviors. In a Data Engineering context, Census refers to the periodic collection and processing of large datasets to update a centralized repository.
what_you_will_learn: Data Ingestion — designing pipelines to collect data from various sources (e.g., databases, APIs, files) into a centralized location
Data Processing — applying transformations and aggregations to raw data to create meaningful insights and metrics
Data Quality — implementing checks and validation rules to ensure data accuracy and consistency across datasets
Data Governance — defining policies and procedures for data access, security, and compliance with regulatory requirements
ETL vs Reverse ETL — understanding the differences between traditional Extract-Transform-Load (ETL) processes and modern Reverse ETL approaches
Census Data Modeling — designing data structures and schemas to efficiently store and query large datasets
Data Warehousing — building a centralized repository for storing, managing, and analyzing census data

knowledge.what_it_is: Segment is a customer data platform that enables Data Engineers to collect, unify, and activate customer data across multiple sources and destinations.
what_you_will_learn: Segment API Keys — using Segment's API keys for authentication and authorization in client-side applications
Sources — setting up and managing data sources such as webhooks, APIs, and databases within Segment
Destinations — configuring and sending data to various destinations like marketing automation tools, CRM systems, and analytics platforms
Traits — defining and tracking customer attributes and behaviors through Segment's trait system
Segments — creating and managing dynamic segments of customers based on specific conditions and criteria
Webhooks — using Segment's webhooks feature for real-time data synchronization between sources and destinations
Server-Side Tracking — implementing server-side tracking with Segment to collect data from server logs, APIs, or other backend systems

knowledge.what_it_is: Machine learning (ML) is a subset of artificial intelligence that enables data engineers to develop predictive models and algorithms that can learn from data, without being explicitly programmed. In the context of data engineering, ML is used for tasks such as data preprocessing, feature engineering, model selection, and deployment.
what_you_will_learn: Supervised learning — training models on labeled datasets to make predictions: regression, classification, clustering
Unsupervised learning — discovering patterns in unlabeled data: dimensionality reduction, anomaly detection, clustering
Model evaluation metrics — understanding accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, ROC-AUC, mean squared error, R-squared
Hyperparameter tuning — using techniques such as grid search, random search, Bayesian optimization to optimize model performance
Overfitting and underfitting — recognizing when models are too complex or too simple for the problem at hand
Regularization techniques — L1, L2 regularization, dropout, early stopping to prevent overfitting
Gradient boosting — ensemble learning method that combines multiple weak models to create a strong predictive model
Neural networks — multilayer perceptrons, convolutional neural networks (CNNs), recurrent neural networks (RNNs)
Model selection — choosing between linear regression, decision trees, random forests, support vector machines (SVMs)
Data preprocessing techniques — handling missing values, encoding categorical variables, scaling/normalizing data
Feature engineering — selecting relevant features, creating new features through transformations and aggregations

knowledge.what_it_is: MLOps (Machine Learning Operations) is the set of practices and tools that enable the deployment, monitoring, and maintenance of machine learning models in production environments. As a Data Engineer, you will be responsible for implementing MLOps pipelines to ensure model reliability, scalability, and efficiency.
what_you_will_learn: Model Serving — deploying trained models as APIs or web services using frameworks like TensorFlow Serving, AWS SageMaker, or Azure Machine Learning
Model Monitoring — tracking model performance metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score using tools like Prometheus, Grafana, or New Relic

Automated Model Re-training — scheduling periodic re-training of models based on changes in data distribution or performance degradation using tools like Apache Airflow, AWS Step Functions, or Azure Data Factory
Model Versioning — managing multiple versions of models and tracking their performance using tools like Git, SVN, or model versioning libraries like MLflow or DVC
Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) pipelines for MLOps — automating the build, test, and deployment of machine learning code using tools like Jenkins, Travis CI, or CircleCI
Model Explainability — providing insights into how models make predictions using techniques like SHAP, LIME, or feature importance analysis
Data Validation and Preprocessing — ensuring data quality and consistency for model training and serving using libraries like Pandas, NumPy, or Scikit-learn
Hyperparameter Tuning — optimizing model performance by tuning hyperparameters using tools like Hyperopt, Optuna, or Ray Tune
Model Deployment to Cloud Platforms — deploying models to cloud platforms such as AWS, Azure, Google Cloud, or IBM Cloud using their respective MLOps services
